learningObjectives: Identify the six major project constraints: Scope, Schedule, Cost, Quality, Resources, and Risk
Understand how constraints interact and create trade-offs (changing one affects others)
Apply constraint trade-off analysis to real-world scenarios
Make informed decisions when stakeholders demand competing priorities
Recognize the expanded constraint model (beyond the traditional triple constraint)

content.headline: The Impossible Project: Six Competing Constraints
content.intro: The Food Festival booth project has a crisis: Fixed deadline (July 4th), NEW health requirements (+$4.5K scope), AND Ava just CUT the budget by $5K. Carlos demands: 'Make it ALL work.' But the math doesn't add up.

failure_cards.title: The Perfect Storm
failure_cards.details: Problem 1: Festival date is FIXED (July 4th). Problem 2: New health requirements mandated (+$4,500 in equipment). Problem 3: Ava just cut the budget from $20K to $15K. Alex does the math: 'We're $9,500 short!'

failure_cards.title: The Impossible Math
failure_cards.details: Alex at the whiteboard: 'Needed: $24.5K. Available: $15K. Gap: $9.5K.' Carlos says: 'Just figure it out. We need EVERYTHING.' Alex: 'Carlos, that's MATHEMATICALLY IMPOSSIBLE!'

failure_cards.title: The Trade-Off Moment
failure_cards.details: Dr. Foster arrives: 'Alex is right. You can't optimize SIX constraints simultaneously.' Maya adds: 'One thing is NON-NEGOTIABLE: food safety. We can't compromise quality.' Alex realizes: 'We need to decide what to FLEX to protect what we CAN'T.'

character_quote.name: Carlos Mendez
character_quote.quote: Alex, we need to launch on July 4th (schedule), with ALL the new health features (scope), under $15K (cost), with perfect food safety (quality), using Maya part-time (resources), and zero risk of contamination. Make it happen.

content.learning_hook: **Every project faces COMPETING CONSTRAINTS.** When you change one, others are affected. Great PMs don't promise 'we can do it all.' They help stakeholders make INFORMED TRADE-OFFS.

interaction.title: Analyze Project Constraints
interaction.description: Dr. Amelia Foster presents the constraint framework. Analyze the Food Festival Booth project and identify which constraints you can adjust.

questions.question: Drag each factor to its constraint category:
questions.situation: Match each project factor to the correct constraint category.

dropZones.label: SCHEDULE
dropZones.detail: When the project must be completed

dropZones.label: RISK
dropZones.detail: Threats that could impact the project

questions.explanation: July 4th = Schedule. Health features = Scope. $15K = Cost. Food safety = Quality. Maya's time = Resources. Contamination = Risk.

correctFeedback.text: July 4th = Schedule. Health features = Scope. $15K = Cost. Food safety = Quality. Maya's time = Resources. Contamination = Risk.
correctFeedback.characterQuote: Understanding which constraint each factor belongs to is the first step in making informed trade-offs.

incorrectFeedback.fallbackText: Ask yourself: Is this about WHAT we deliver, WHEN we deliver, or HOW MUCH we spend?
incorrectFeedback.characterQuote: Ask yourself: Is this about WHAT we deliver, WHEN we deliver, or HOW MUCH we spend?

questions.retryHint: Match each factor to what it constrains: deliverables (scope), timeline (schedule), budget (cost), standards (quality), availability (resources), or threats (risk).

questions.question: What is the BEST way to make this project feasible?
questions.situation: Schedule (July 4th) is FIXED. Quality (food safety) is FIXED. Cost is CONSTRAINED ($15K). Scope INCREASED (+$4.5K). Total gap: $9.5K shortfall.

options.text: Cancel the project — it's impossible
options.whyWrong: Cancelling avoids the problem but doesn't solve it. Good PMs find alternatives before recommending cancellation.

options.text: Cut scope — prioritize CRITICAL health features, defer nice-to-have features
Convince Ava to restore the $20K budget
options.whyWrong: Ava already made her decision. Assuming you can override an investor's budget constraint is unrealistic.

options.text: Delay to August festival
options.whyWrong: The July 4th date is NON-NEGOTIABLE. The festival organizer controls the schedule, not Savory & Co.

options.text: Use cheaper equipment that doesn't fully meet health code
options.whyWrong: Reducing QUALITY (compliance) is NEVER acceptable when regulatory requirements are involved. This creates legal risk.

options.text: Have Maya work overtime to do everything
options.whyWrong: Maya is already limited to 20 hrs/week due to operations. More hours would burn out a key resource.

questions.explanation: When schedule and quality are FIXED and cost is CONSTRAINED, the only flexible constraint is SCOPE. Prioritize CRITICAL features (handwashing = highest food safety impact), defer lower-priority features.

correctFeedback.text: When schedule and quality are FIXED and cost is CONSTRAINED, the only flexible constraint is SCOPE. Prioritize CRITICAL features (handwashing = highest food safety impact), defer lower-priority features.
correctFeedback.characterQuote: I didn't want to cut ANY features. But I see now — handwashing stations are critical for safety. Sneeze guards can wait.

incorrectFeedback.fallbackText: We can't change the date or compromise safety. We can't get more money. What CAN we change?
incorrectFeedback.characterQuote: We can't change the date or compromise safety. We can't get more money. What CAN we change?

questions.retryHint: Fixed: Schedule, Quality. Constrained: Cost. What's the only thing LEFT to adjust?

questions.question: Match each stakeholder to their primary constraint priority:
questions.situation: Different stakeholders prioritize different constraints. Match each stakeholder to their TOP priority.
questions.explanation: Ava (investor) prioritizes Cost. Festival Organizer prioritizes Schedule. Maya (chef) prioritizes Quality. Understanding stakeholder priorities helps negotiate trade-offs.

correctFeedback.text: Ava (investor) prioritizes Cost. Festival Organizer prioritizes Schedule. Maya (chef) prioritizes Quality. Understanding stakeholder priorities helps negotiate trade-offs.
correctFeedback.characterQuote: Know your stakeholders' priorities. That's how you find trade-offs everyone can accept.

incorrectFeedback.fallbackText: What matters most to each person based on their role?
incorrectFeedback.characterQuote: What matters most to each person based on their role?

questions.retryHint: Investor = money matters. Festival organizer = date matters. Chef = food safety matters.

tabs.title: Triple Constraint
tabs.subtitle: The Iron Triangle
tabs.content:
**The Classic PM Model:**

Every project balances three core constraints:

**SCOPE** — What is delivered?
**TIME** — When is it delivered?
**COST** — How much does it cost?

**The Iron Triangle Rule:**
'Good, Fast, Cheap — Pick TWO.'

- Good + Fast = Expensive
- Good + Cheap = Slow
- Fast + Cheap = Poor Quality

**Key Insight:** You CANNOT optimize all three simultaneously. Trade-offs are inevitable.

tabs.title: Six Constraints
tabs.subtitle: Expanded Model
tabs.content:
**Modern PMI View:** Projects face MORE than three constraints.

**1. SCOPE** — What deliverables/features?
**2. SCHEDULE** — When must it be completed?
**3. COST** — What's the budget?
**4. QUALITY** — What standards must be met?
**5. RESOURCES** — What people/equipment are available?
**6. RISK** — What threats could impact success?

**The Interconnection:**
- Increase Scope → Increase Cost and/or Schedule
- Decrease Schedule → Increase Cost OR Decrease Quality
- Decrease Cost → Decrease Scope OR Increase Schedule

tabs.title: Trade-Off Steps
tabs.subtitle: Five-Step Framework
tabs.content:
**How to Manage Competing Constraints:**

**Step 1: Identify ALL Constraints**
Don't assume just scope/time/cost — check all six

**Step 2: Determine FIXED vs FLEXIBLE**
- Fixed: Non-negotiable (regulatory, investor limits)
- Flexible: Can be negotiated

**Step 3: Prioritize with Stakeholders**
Ask: 'Which is MOST important? Which can we compromise?'

**Step 4: Analyze Trade-Offs**
For each option, assess impact on ALL constraints

**Step 5: Document Decisions**
Record what was decided, why, and what was sacrificed

tabs.title: Common Scenarios
tabs.subtitle: Trade-Off Examples
tabs.content:
**Scenario: Stakeholder Demands Faster Delivery**
- Options: Increase Cost (overtime), Decrease Scope (MVP), Increase Risk (less testing)
- Response: 'We can deliver faster if we increase budget OR reduce scope.'

**Scenario: Stakeholder Cuts Budget**
- Options: Decrease Scope, Increase Schedule
- Response: 'With less budget, we can reduce scope OR extend timeline.'

**Scenario: Stakeholder Adds Features**
- Options: Increase Cost, Increase Schedule, Swap features
- Response: 'Adding features will increase budget OR delay delivery.'

tabs.title: Never Compromise
tabs.subtitle: What's Non-Negotiable
tabs.content:
**Some constraints should NEVER be compromised:**

**Quality (Regulatory/Safety)**
- Health codes, safety standards, legal compliance
- Cutting corners creates liability and reputational risk

**Security (Customer Data)**
- Payment processing, personal information
- Breaches are catastrophic for trust and business

**Ethics (Honesty)**
- Don't hide problems or misrepresent progress
- Transparency builds trust, deception destroys it

**Key Principle:** When Quality/Safety are fixed, adjust SCOPE or SCHEDULE instead — NEVER cut corners on compliance.

scenario.title: Online Ordering System Launch
scenario.description: Savory & Co. is launching an online ordering system. Original plan: 4 months, $120K, full features. NOW: Carlos wants to launch in 2 months because a competitor just launched. > Carlos demands: 'Launch in 2 months, not 4 — our competitor just launched!'

images.alt: Carlos pressuring Alex about accelerated deadline

details: **Original:** 4 months, $120K, full system (order, payment, tracking, loyalty, reviews)
**Fixed:** Quality (99.9% uptime, PCI compliance for payments)

questions.question: What constraint is Carlos changing?
questions.situation: Carlos just learned that a competitor launched their online ordering system. He demands that Savory & Co.'s system launch in 2 months instead of the original 4-month timeline.

options.text: Scope (adding features)
options.whyWrong: Carlos isn't adding features — he's accelerating the TIMELINE. That's Schedule, not Scope.

options.text: Schedule (accelerating deadline from 4 months to 2 months)
Cost (reducing budget)
options.whyWrong: Carlos didn't mention budget. He's compressing the TIMELINE.

options.text: Quality (lowering standards)
options.whyWrong: Carlos didn't say lower quality. He wants FASTER delivery — that's Schedule.

questions.explanation: Carlos is compressing the SCHEDULE from 4 months to 2 months. This creates pressure on other constraints.

correctFeedback.text: Carlos is compressing the SCHEDULE from 4 months to 2 months. This creates pressure on other constraints.
correctFeedback.characterQuote: Our competitor just launched. We need to move FASTER. 4 months is too slow now.

incorrectFeedback.fallbackText: I'm not asking for more features or less money. I'm asking for it SOONER.
incorrectFeedback.characterQuote: I'm not asking for more features or less money. I'm asking for it SOONER.

questions.retryHint: Carlos wants the project done in 2 months instead of 4. What constraint is that?

questions.question: Given the accelerated schedule (2 months instead of 4), what is the BEST trade-off?
questions.situation: The online ordering project schedule has been compressed from 4 months to 2 months. Quality is fixed (99.9% uptime, PCI compliance). The original scope includes order, payment, tracking, loyalty, and reviews features.

options.text: Increase cost — pay vendor 50% premium for rush delivery (+$60K)
options.whyWrong: Ava is unlikely to approve a 50% budget increase ($180K total). Cost constraint is likely fixed.

options.text: Reduce scope — launch MVP (order + payment only), add loyalty and reviews in Phase 2
Reduce quality — skip security testing to save time
options.whyWrong: NEVER acceptable for payment systems. PCI compliance is NON-NEGOTIABLE. A breach would be catastrophic.

options.text: Tell Carlos it's impossible — refuse to accelerate
options.whyWrong: Refusing doesn't solve the competitive pressure. Good PMs propose alternatives, not just refuse.

questions.explanation: When schedule is compressed, reduce SCOPE to core value (MVP). Order + payment delivers business value; loyalty and reviews can wait.

correctFeedback.text: When schedule is compressed, reduce SCOPE to core value (MVP). Order + payment delivers business value; loyalty and reviews can wait.
correctFeedback.characterQuote: Launching basic ordering in 2 months beats launching everything in 4 months — after the competitor already owns the market.

incorrectFeedback.fallbackText: We can't skip security. We probably can't double the budget. What CAN we flex?
incorrectFeedback.characterQuote: We can't skip security. We probably can't double the budget. What CAN we flex?

questions.retryHint: Schedule compressed, quality fixed, cost likely fixed. What's left to adjust?

questions.question: How should Alex communicate this trade-off decision to Carlos?
questions.situation: Alex has decided to propose an MVP approach — launching with order and payment features only in 2 months, deferring loyalty and reviews to Phase 2. Now Alex needs to present this trade-off to Carlos.

options.text: 'We can't do 2 months. It's impossible.'
options.whyWrong: Refusing without alternatives isn't helpful. PMs should present OPTIONS, not just say no.

options.text: '2 months is feasible if we launch MVP (order + payment). We'll add loyalty and reviews in Phase 2 after launch.'
'I'll figure it out somehow. Don't worry about the details.'
options.whyWrong: Vague promises without clear trade-offs set up for failure. Be transparent about what's changing.

options.text: 'We can do it if you approve $60K extra budget.'
options.whyWrong: Jumping to cost increase without exploring scope options isn't strategic. MVP is a better first option.

questions.explanation: Great communication! You proposed a clear trade-off: reduced scope (MVP) to meet the accelerated schedule, with a plan for Phase 2.

correctFeedback.text: Great communication! You proposed a clear trade-off: reduced scope (MVP) to meet the accelerated schedule, with a plan for Phase 2.
correctFeedback.characterQuote: This is exactly how to communicate trade-offs: what we're flexing, what we're protecting, and the path forward.

incorrectFeedback.fallbackText: Be specific. What CAN you deliver in 2 months? What comes later?
incorrectFeedback.characterQuote: Be specific. What CAN you deliver in 2 months? What comes later?

questions.retryHint: Effective trade-off communication: 'We CAN do X if we adjust Y. Here's the plan...'

content.title: Lesson Complete!
content.summary: You've learned that projects face SIX major constraints — Scope, Schedule, Cost, Quality, Resources, and Risk. These constraints are INTERCONNECTED: changing one affects others. Great PMs help stakeholders make INFORMED TRADE-OFFS based on priorities.
key_takeaways: SIX MAJOR CONSTRAINTS: Scope, Schedule, Cost, Quality, Resources, Risk
IRON TRIANGLE RULE: 'Good, Fast, Cheap — Pick Two.' Can't optimize all simultaneously.
CONSTRAINTS ARE INTERCONNECTED: Increase scope → increase cost/schedule. Decrease schedule → increase cost or decrease quality.
FIXED vs FLEXIBLE: Identify which constraints CAN'T change vs CAN change.
TRADE-OFF FRAMEWORK: Identify → Determine fixed/flexible → Prioritize → Analyze → Document
NEVER COMPROMISE: Quality when regulatory/safety involved. Security when customer data involved.

next_lesson.title: Project Success & Value
next_lesson.description: Learn what defines project success — beyond just meeting scope, time, and cost.

content.title: PMP Practice Questions
content.description: Test your knowledge with real PMP-style questions

questions.question: What concept BEST describes the relationship between schedule, budget, and scope in this scenario?
questions.situation: A restaurant chain is building a new flagship location. The project is on track for a June 1 completion date. The CEO announces that a major food critic will visit on May 15 — two weeks earlier than planned. The CEO demands the project be completed by May 15.

options.text: Scope creep requiring change control
options.whyWrong: Scope creep is unauthorized scope expansion. This scenario is about compressing the SCHEDULE, not expanding scope.

options.text: Triple constraint trade-offs where changing one constraint affects others
Risk response planning for schedule uncertainty
options.whyWrong: Risk response would address potential threats. This is a known constraint change requiring trade-off analysis.

options.text: Resource leveling to balance workload
options.whyWrong: Resource leveling adjusts resource assignments. This scenario is about constraint trade-offs.

questions.explanation: The triple constraint shows that changing one constraint typically requires adjusting others. Compressing schedule by 2 weeks requires either more budget (overtime) or less scope (fewer features).

correctFeedback.text: The triple constraint shows that changing one constraint typically requires adjusting others. Compressing schedule by 2 weeks requires either more budget (overtime) or less scope (fewer features).
correctFeedback.characterQuote: You can't compress time for free. Something else must flex.

questions.retryHint: The scenario describes changing schedule and its impact on other constraints. What concept explains this?

questions.question: How should the project manager respond to this suggestion?
questions.situation: A catering company is preparing for a high-profile corporate event. The project is over budget due to unexpected ingredient cost increases. One team member suggests using lower-quality ingredients that still meet minimum food safety standards but don't match the premium quality promised to the client.

options.text: Accept the suggestion since the ingredients still meet safety standards
options.whyWrong: Meeting minimum safety is not the same as meeting the premium quality PROMISED to the client. This would violate the quality baseline.

options.text: Reject the suggestion and inform stakeholders that quality compromises require client approval
Implement the change without informing the client to stay within budget
options.whyWrong: Changing quality without client approval violates transparency. The client has a right to know.

options.text: Escalate immediately to senior management to override the budget constraint
options.whyWrong: Escalation may be appropriate, but first the PM should present options to the client.

questions.explanation: Quality cannot be unilaterally changed without stakeholder approval. The PM should present options transparently: pay more for premium quality, or approve quality reduction.

correctFeedback.text: Quality cannot be unilaterally changed without stakeholder approval. The PM should present options transparently: pay more for premium quality, or approve quality reduction.
correctFeedback.characterQuote: Never compromise quality without the client's knowledge. That's how you lose trust.

questions.retryHint: Quality changes affect what the client receives. Who should approve such changes?

questions.question: Which constraint is MOST DIRECTLY affected by this situation?
questions.situation: A project to renovate a restaurant's kitchen has been progressing on schedule. The project manager learns that the lead electrician — a critical resource — must leave the project for two weeks due to a family emergency. No qualified replacement is available locally.

options.text: Scope — the project will deliver fewer features
options.whyWrong: The scope isn't necessarily affected. The constraint is about RESOURCES and its impact on SCHEDULE.

options.text: Cost — the project will exceed budget
options.whyWrong: Cost might be a secondary impact (if you pay premium for replacement), but the PRIMARY constraint is Resources/Schedule.

options.text: Resources — a critical team member is unavailable, likely impacting schedule
Quality — the electrical work will be substandard
options.whyWrong: Quality isn't automatically affected — when the electrician returns, work will meet standards. The issue is availability.

questions.explanation: The electrician's absence is a RESOURCE constraint directly impacting SCHEDULE. The PM should assess options: delay tasks, find replacement, or accept schedule slip.

correctFeedback.text: The electrician's absence is a RESOURCE constraint directly impacting SCHEDULE. The PM should assess options: delay tasks, find replacement, or accept schedule slip.
correctFeedback.characterQuote: When resources aren't available, schedule usually takes the hit — unless you can find alternatives.

questions.retryHint: The electrician is a critical RESOURCE. When resources are unavailable, which constraint is typically affected?

questions.question: What should the project manager do FIRST?
questions.situation: A hospitality company is launching a new hotel restaurant. The project sponsor (owner) prioritizes staying within the $2M budget. The general manager prioritizes opening by December 1 (holiday season). The executive chef prioritizes premium kitchen equipment for quality. Meeting all three priorities is not possible.

options.text: Make the trade-off decision based on project management best practices
options.whyWrong: The PM shouldn't make constraint trade-off decisions unilaterally. Stakeholders should be involved in prioritization.

options.text: Facilitate a discussion with stakeholders to understand priorities and agree on trade-offs
Escalate to the company CEO to make the final decision
options.whyWrong: Escalating before facilitating discussion skips the PM's role. First help stakeholders understand trade-offs.

options.text: Delay the decision until more information is available
options.whyWrong: Delaying doesn't resolve the conflict. The PM should proactively facilitate trade-off discussions.

questions.explanation: When stakeholders have competing priorities, the PM facilitates discussion, presents trade-off options, and helps stakeholders agree on priorities. The PM doesn't make the final call on business priorities.

correctFeedback.text: When stakeholders have competing priorities, the PM facilitates discussion, presents trade-off options, and helps stakeholders agree on priorities. The PM doesn't make the final call on business priorities.
correctFeedback.characterQuote: My job as sponsor is to set priorities. Alex's job is to show me the options and trade-offs.

questions.retryHint: The PM's role is to facilitate, not decide. What should the PM do with competing stakeholder priorities?
